title: Sunscreen on White Cotton
timeEstimate: 20–60 minutes; oil + mineral combo, NEVER chlorine bleach (locks yellow permanently)
stainChemistry: Sunscreen is three things stuck together: oil base (mineral oil, silicone), UV filters (chemical blockers like avobenzone), and mineral blockers (titanium dioxide or zinc oxide — visible white residue). On white cotton, the oil + mineral combo is tractable with the standard powder + Dawn + OxiClean sequence. The trap is chlorine bleach — it reacts with avobenzone to produce a permanent yellow stain that's WORSE than the original. This is the rare case where white cotton's usual chlorine-bleach option is OFF the table. Cotton with elastane in trim, collars, or waistbands also bans chlorine for a different reason (chlorine destroys elastane permanently). Hot water is also an enemy here — accelerates the avobenzone-iron yellow. Cool through chemistry, hot wash AFTER if needed and care label permits.
whyThisWorks: Powder preload pulls bulk oil up before water spreads it. Generous Dawn handles the oily film. OxiClean cool soak handles residual yellow chromophore from avobenzone-iron reaction. 3% peroxide finishes any remaining shadow. The protocol stops at oxygen bleach (OxiClean / peroxide) — chlorine bleach would lock the avobenzone yellow permanently. White cotton tolerates the rest of the sequence widely, but the chlorine boundary is hard for sunscreen specifically.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): sunscreen is mineral oil / silicone carrier + chemical UV filters (avobenzone, oxybenzone, octinoxate) + physical blockers (TiO₂, ZnO) + emulsifiers. Avobenzone forms ferric complexes with iron in water — the source of the rust-yellow ring. Chlorine + avobenzone produces a more stable colored complex that resists further bleaching. Pro uses NSD + 3% H₂O₂ + sodium hydrosulfite for set yellow; chlorine bleach is BANNED on sunscreen residue.
defaultAssumption: Beach day / pool day / sport sunscreen rub-off on a white cotton t-shirt, swim cover-up, or athletic-wear. Fresh = quick win with Dawn alone. Aged yellow ring (chemical sunscreen + iron-rich water + heat exposure) = full sequence ending at peroxide — NEVER chlorine bleach.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover the spot with cornstarch or baking soda. Press gently. 20–30 min absorb. Soft-brush off.
Generous Dawn directly on the spot. Soft toothbrush 10 min. White cotton tolerates warm Dawn. Damp-cloth rinse.

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean per label, cool water, soak basin
spottingProtocol.instruction: OxiClean cool soak 30–60 min for residual avobenzone-iron yellow on white cotton. Oxygen bleach is safe here; chlorine bleach is BANNED on sunscreen.

spottingProtocol.technique: Direct peroxide pass for set avobenzone-iron yellow
spottingProtocol.equipment: 3% H₂O₂ on damp cloth or as soak additive
spottingProtocol.instruction: Stubborn yellow ring: 3% peroxide damp cloth 10 min, OR add 1/2 cup to OxiClean soak. NEVER mix with chlorine bleach — toxic fumes AND locks avobenzone yellow permanently.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, cool water, normal detergent
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. NEVER chlorine bleach — locks avobenzone-iron yellow permanently and is more visible than the original stain. AIR-DRY only.

steps: 1. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
2. Generous Dawn pretreat, warm, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
3. OxiClean cool soak 30–60 min for residual yellow.
4. 3% H₂O₂ damp cloth or soak additive for stubborn yellow.
5. Cool wash + AIR-DRY. NEVER chlorine bleach on sunscreen residue.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat accelerates avobenzone-iron yellow
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach on sunscreen residue — locks avobenzone-iron yellow permanently (worse than original stain)
NEVER hot water — accelerates the yellow ring
NEVER mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + OxiClean — toxic fumes
Powder preload BEFORE water — water-first spreads oil
Oxygen bleach (OxiClean) is the chlorine alternative on sunscreen — safe on white cotton
Cotton with elastane (modern athletic wear): chlorine also banned for elastane reasons

homeSolutions.agent: Sunscreen Has a Special Rule on White Cotton — NO Chlorine Bleach
homeSolutions.instruction: White cotton usually tolerates chlorine bleach as the home nuclear option for stubborn stains. Sunscreen is the exception. Chlorine + avobenzone (the chemical UV filter in most sunscreens) produces a permanent yellow stain that's WORSE than the original. The home protocol stops at oxygen bleach (OxiClean / 3% peroxide). The sequence: powder preload → Dawn → OxiClean cool soak → 3% peroxide. Never chlorine.

homeSolutions.agent: Cornstarch or Baking Soda Preload — 20 to 30 Minutes
homeSolutions.instruction: Cover the spot with cornstarch or baking soda. Press gently. 20 to 30 minutes. Soft-brush off. Most surface oil and mineral residue come off this way without water.
Generous Dawn directly on the spot. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 10 minutes. White cotton tolerates warm (warm, not hot) water for the Dawn step. Damp-cloth rinse.
Mix OxiClean per label in COOL water in a basin. Submerge 30 to 60 minutes. Handles residual yellow on white cotton. Oxygen bleach is safe; chlorine bleach is the chemistry trap.

If the yellow ring persists: 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp cloth, press 10 minutes, blot. OR add 1/2 cup to the OxiClean soak. Do NOT mix peroxide with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or any other oxidizer — toxic fumes AND locks the yellow.

Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. NEVER reach for chlorine bleach on sunscreen residue — chlorine + avobenzone creates a permanent yellow that's MORE visible than the original stain. This is the rare case where white cotton's usual chlorine-bleach option is off the table.

homeSolutions.agent: Switch to Mineral Sunscreen for Repeat Garments
homeSolutions.instruction: If you keep getting yellow rings on white shirts, the cause is the chemical UV filter avobenzone in your sunscreen reacting with iron in your water. Mineral-only sunscreens (pure zinc oxide / titanium dioxide, no avobenzone or oxybenzone) leave white residue but don't produce the yellow lock. Switch sunscreens for swim cover-ups and white shirts you want to keep white.

steps: 1. NEVER chlorine bleach on sunscreen — locks yellow permanently.
2. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
3. Generous Dawn pretreat, warm, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
5. 3% peroxide damp cloth or soak additive for stubborn yellow.
7. Prevention: mineral-only sunscreen for white garments.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if chlorine bleach was already used (produced permanent yellow — pro is the only escalation, and even then unreliable). Stop if hot dryer was used on yellow ring (often plateau). Stop after full sequence (preload + Dawn + OxiClean + peroxide) without progress.
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach on sunscreen — locks avobenzone-iron yellow permanently
NEVER mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + OxiClean
Cotton with elastane: chlorine also banned for elastane reasons (destroys elastane)

neverDo: Never chlorine bleach on sunscreen residue — locks avobenzone-iron yellow permanently
Never mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + OxiClean — toxic fumes
Never hot water on sunscreen residue — accelerates yellow
Never iron over a yellow ring — heat sets it
Never chlorine bleach on cotton with elastane — destroys elastane
fiberSensitivities: White cotton (no elastane, no trim): tolerates Dawn, OxiClean, 3% peroxide — but NOT chlorine bleach on sunscreen
White cotton with elastane (athletic wear, modern shirts): chlorine also banned for elastane reasons
Avobenzone (chemical sunscreen UV filter): the polyester/cotton lock chemical — chlorine reaction is irreversible
Mineral sunscreens (zinc / titanium dioxide only): leave white residue but no yellow lock — easier

materialWarnings: Sunscreen is the rare case where chlorine bleach is BANNED on white cotton
Avobenzone + chlorine = permanent yellow worse than original
Powder preload before water — water-first spreads oil
No hot water — accelerates yellow ring

escalation.when: Chlorine bleach was already used on sunscreen (produced permanent yellow — even pro reducing-bleach chemistry is unreliable on this lock). Hot dryer used on yellow ring (often plateau). Full sequence (preload + Dawn + OxiClean + peroxide) plateau. Cotton with elastane and yellow buildup (treatment options narrow).
escalation.whatToTell: "Sunscreen on [solid white / white-with-trim / white-with-elastane / white blend] cotton, [chemical (avobenzone) / mineral (zinc / titanium) / mixed], age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [cornstarch preload + Dawn warm + OxiClean cool soak + 3% peroxide + cool wash]. NEVER chlorine bleach. [Residual yellow / chlorine reaction occurred / hot-dried suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with reducing-bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) chemistry — note: even pro chemistry is unreliable on chlorine-locked avobenzone yellow

contextQuestions: Chemical sunscreen (avobenzone, oxybenzone) or mineral (zinc / titanium dioxide) or mixed?
Solid white cotton, white with colored trim, white with elastane, or white blend?
Fresh oil residue or aged yellow ring?
Has chlorine bleach been used on this stain (often locks the yellow)?
Well water or city water (well water has more iron, more avobenzone reaction)?
Has hot water or hot dryer been applied?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Avobenzone-iron yellow is the polyester-and-cotton-specific lock from chemical sunscreens. Chlorine bleach reaction is the most common home failure mode — instinctive (white cotton + bleach) and irreversible. Mineral-only sunscreens (pure zinc oxide / titanium dioxide) are the prevention path because they don't have avobenzone. Well water households see this more often because well water has more iron. Pro reducing-bleach is the escalation, but even pro chemistry is unreliable on chlorine-locked avobenzone yellow — manage expectations.
